story:
Once upon a time, in a land far away, the kingdom of Aradia flourished under Queen Elena, a just and gentle ruler whose wisdom softened every hard season. Though loved by all, Elena carried a quiet sorrow: her noble husband had died years before, and no second bond could replace the first. She poured her grief into service, seeing to the needs of her people before her own.

When the dragon Kargath descended, that peace shattered. Its scales were hard as forged steel, its breath melted iron, and whole fields blazed wherever its shadow passed. Elena called her advisors, rangers, and captains, yet no stratagem could pierce the dragon’s hide. Hope thinned like smoke.

Refusing despair, Elena sought counsel from the kingdom’s wise women. They recalled an old prophecy: not a prince or a knight, but a true-hearted maker would bring the weapon that fate required. Skeptical yet resolute, Elena proclaimed across Aradia that any who dared face Kargath might come forth.

Many brave warriors answered. Lances splintered, arrows hissed, and shields charred, but the dragon scarcely noticed. When courage seemed spent, a young blacksmith named Liam petitioned the queen. He was no knight, he said, but he knew craft and metal. “Your Majesty,” he added softly, “as your wise women recalled to you: not a prince or a knight, but a true-hearted maker would bring the weapon that fate required. I am that maker.” If granted time, a hot forge, rare ores, and the wise women’s blessing, he would attempt a spear to find the one place Kargath could be harmed.

Trusting both the prophecy and the steadiness in his voice, Elena agreed. For days and nights the forge burned. Liam folded a special alloy until it sang, tempered it bright, and ground a tip so fine it flashed like frost. At the anvil’s hush, the wise women etched runes along the shaft and bound spells of strength, truer aim, and steadfast heart—not to slay for glory, but to defend the innocent. When the work was done, the spear felt alive in the hand, simple to behold and impossible to break.

Presented in the great hall, the weapon drew a collective breath from the court. Elena ordered no more delay. On open ground where the hills met the river, Kargath came roaring, fire boiling the air. Liam did not charge; he waited. He watched the beat of wing and coil of muscle, counting the breath between flames. When the dragon folded forward to strike, its chest bared for an instant. Liam cast the spear with all his strength and all the blessings laid upon it. Light raced its course. The point found the hidden path to the heart.

Kargath crashed to earth, the fire gone from its throat. The people of Aradia wept and cheered, and Queen Elena named Liam a hero. She offered him place and honor at her side as advisor and protector. Liam bowed and thanked her, but he asked instead to return to his forge and make what people needed—plows, hinges, and, when called for, wonders. Elena, who understood love and loss and chosen callings, let him go with gifts and the kingdom’s gratitude. Peace returned to Aradia, and the queen’s sorrow grew lighter in the warmth of her people’s joy.
